text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing a large left-sided ovarian cyst adjacent to the uterus and sigmoid colon. The pelvis appears normal. The ovarian cyst is supported by pulling the infundibulopelvic ligament with the left hand. An aspiration needle is used to aspirate the cystic content, facilitating its deflation and easier removal. The cyst is gradually separated, and the surgeon uses a monopolar cautery or harmonic device to cut along the anti-meso-ovarian border.

The surgeon holds the outer ovarian cortex with the left hand while using the right hand to strip the cyst from the surrounding tissue. This stripping technique is repeated multiple times, gradually separating the cyst, which is now almost completely detached and coming out easily.

The surgeon completes the removal of the ovarian cyst, ensuring the outer ovarian cortex remains healthy. The uterus appears normal, and the remaining ovarian pocket is checked for bleeding. Repeated irrigation and suction are performed to clean the surgical area, leaving some fluid inside the pelvis. The patient is positioned with the head elevated to prevent fluid from moving to the upper abdomen.

The surgeon uses a 5mm umbilical port to remove the intact ovarian cyst, utilizing a cannula to directly suck and pull the cyst out without an endo bag. Ringer lactate is used for its anti-adhesive properties during the procedure.
